comment_text.snp5.1:1.3: Ākiñcaññaṁ (“nothingness”) refers to “the dimension of nothingness”, which is an important topic in the chapters to come. It seems he was an acolyte of the Kosalan brahmins such as Āḷāra Kālāma who taught the state of nothingsness as the highest realization, and wanted to find a secluded place to pursue his meditations (MN 26:15.1). The sense of “owning nothing” is also relevant.
comment_text.snp5.1:2.2: The inscription Siri Pulumayi at Nasik supports the spelling muḷaka. (Sylvain Lévi, “Gonarda, The Cradle of the Gonardiya”, Journal Of The Andhra Historical Society, vol.9, pt.1, July 1934, pg. 8.)
comment_text.snp5.1:2.3: This is the only early occurrence of this famed sacred river of the south. It arises near Nashik, Maharashtra, not far from Mumbai on the west coast, and flows east for 1,465 kilometres before emptying in the Bay of Bengal. The name godāvarī means “granter of cows” due to the abundance the river brings to the lands through which it flows. The town at its source was called Govardhana, “cow-prosperity”. Normally spelled godhāvarī in Pali, the form godāvarī is standard in Sanskrit, including Buddhist texts (eg. Mahāvastu 3.363) and it seems the Pali spelling is an error.
comment_text.snp5.1:8.4: The threat of losing one’s head is found at eg. Chāndogya Upaniṣad 1.8.6 and Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.3.24, or at 3.9.26 when it actually did fall off. I cannot trace the detail of heads being split in seven to any early Sanskrit texts, but it is found in later texts such as Rāmāyaṇa 7.26.44c and Mahābhārata 14.7.2c.
comment_text.snp5.1:15.2: Note that, in the EBTs, pathavimaṇḍala primarily refers to the region ruled by a Wheel-Turning Monarch. Thus it doesn’t mean “circle” in the sense of “the circle of the earth” but rather a “sphere of influence”, i.e. “territory”.
comment_text.snp5.1:16.1: Purā can be either “from the city” (Norman), or “formerly” (commentary, followed by Bodhi and Jayawickrama). I think the poet is deliberately echoing the opening line of the text, where it must mean “from the city”.
comment_text.snp5.1:17.4: “He is the one of clear eye regarding all things.”
comment_text.snp5.1:21.1: Mandira is unusual and probably a sign of lateness. Bodhi has “realm”, Norman “city”, but the normal meaning in Sanskrit is a “dwelling place”, and in the Jātakas it is always used in the sense of a home.
comment_text.snp5.1:21.3: The commentary gives the senses vigatadhuro “free of burden” (followed by Norman) and appaṭimo “unrivalled” (followed by Bodhi), but at AN 3.20:2.1 it has the sense “indefatigable” and there seems no reason why it shouldn’t have the same meaning here.
comment_text.snp5.1:34.4: One of the many signs of lateness in this passage. Vāsana in this sense is found only in the very latest strata of canonical and semi-canonical texts such as the Milindapañha, where we find pubbe vāsitavāsanā (Mil 6.2.1:3.2).
comment_text.snp5.1:36.2: The Sinhalese commentary explains puri or pura as “former”, while the Burmese commentary explains it as “citadel”. The city continued under this name for many centuries, so “former” seems unlikely. It is modern Maheshwar.
comment_text.snp5.1:36.3: Ujjenī is Ujjain. Gonaddhā is not identified, but must have been near Bhopal.
comment_text.snp5.1:36.4: Vedisa is modern Vidisha. Vanasa is called Tumbavana in both inscriptions and Pali commentaries, so may be the modern Tumen.
comment_text.snp5.1:37.3: Bodhi and Norman have Setavya, but DN 23 shows it is feminine.
comment_text.snp5.1:37.4: See my remarks on mandira above. Kusinārā was famously not a city.
comment_text.snp5.1:51.1: This echoes Chāndogya Upaniṣad 1.10 and 1.11, where Uṣasti, an itinerant brahmin wandering east who has just eaten ritually unclean food, warns priests at a royal sacrifice multiple times that one who recites prayers while ignorant (avidvān) of their meaning will suffer their head falling off.

translation_text.snp5.1:1.1: From the fair city of the Kosalans
translation_text.snp5.1:1.3: came a brahmin expert in hymns,
translation_text.snp5.1:2.1: He settled in a region under the shared dominion
translation_text.snp5.1:2.3: where he lived on the bank of the Godāvarī River,
translation_text.snp5.1:2.4: getting by on gleanings and fruit.
translation_text.snp5.1:3.3: With the revenue earned from there
translation_text.snp5.1:4.1: When he had completed the great sacrifice,
translation_text.snp5.1:4.2: he returned to his hermitage once more.
translation_text.snp5.1:5.2: with grotty teeth and dusty head,
translation_text.snp5.1:5.4: and asked for five hundred coins.
translation_text.snp5.1:6.2: he invited him to sit down,
translation_text.snp5.1:6.3: asked of his happiness and well-being,
translation_text.snp5.1:7.4: I don’t have five hundred coins.”
translation_text.snp5.1:8.4: let your head explode in seven!”
translation_text.snp5.1:9.2: that charlatan uttered his dreadful curse.
translation_text.snp5.1:10.2: stricken by the dart of sorrow.
translation_text.snp5.1:10.3: And in such a state of mind,
translation_text.snp5.1:12.2: about the head, he only wants money.
translation_text.snp5.1:12.3: When it comes to heads or head-splitting,
translation_text.snp5.1:12.4: he has no knowledge at all.”
translation_text.snp5.1:13.3: Let me hear what you say
translation_text.snp5.1:14.2: I have no knowledge in that matter.
translation_text.snp5.1:14.3: When it comes to heads or head-splitting,
translation_text.snp5.1:14.4: it is the Victors who have vision.”
translation_text.snp5.1:15.3: does know about heads and head-splitting?
translation_text.snp5.1:16.2: the World Leader has gone forth.
translation_text.snp5.1:16.3: He is a scion of King Okkāka,
translation_text.snp5.1:17.1: For he, brahmin, is the Awakened One!
translation_text.snp5.1:17.2: He has gone beyond all things;
translation_text.snp5.1:17.3: he has attained to all knowledge and power;
translation_text.snp5.1:17.4: his eye sees clearly in all things,
translation_text.snp5.1:17.5: he has attained the end of all deeds;
translation_text.snp5.1:17.6: he is freed with the ending of attachments.
translation_text.snp5.1:18.1: That Buddha, the Blessed One in the world,
translation_text.snp5.1:19.4: and he was filled to brimming with joy.
translation_text.snp5.1:20.4: or in what land is the protector of the world,
translation_text.snp5.1:20.5: where we may go and pay respects
translation_text.snp5.1:20.6: to the Awakened One, best of men?”
translation_text.snp5.1:21.1: “Near Sāvatthī, home of the Kosalans, <j>is the Victor
translation_text.snp5.1:21.2: abounding in wisdom, vast in intelligence.
translation_text.snp5.1:21.3: That Sakyan is indefatigable, free of defilements,
translation_text.snp5.1:21.4: that captain of men understands head-splitting.”
translation_text.snp5.1:22.2: brahmins who had mastered the hymns:
translation_text.snp5.1:23.1: Today has arisen in the world
translation_text.snp5.1:23.2: one whose appearance in the world
translation_text.snp5.1:23.4: he is renowned as the Awakened One.
translation_text.snp5.1:23.6: and see the best of men.”
translation_text.snp5.1:24.1: “Brahmin, how then are we to know
translation_text.snp5.1:24.2: the Buddha when we see him?
translation_text.snp5.1:24.3: We don’t know, please tell us,
translation_text.snp5.1:25.2: have been handed down in our hymns.
translation_text.snp5.1:26.1: One upon whose body is found
translation_text.snp5.1:26.2: these marks of a great man
translation_text.snp5.1:28.2: from the lay life to homelessness,
translation_text.snp5.1:28.3: he becomes an Awakened One, a perfected one,
translation_text.snp5.1:29.1: Ask him about my birth, clan, and marks,
translation_text.snp5.1:29.2: my hymns and acolytes; and further,
translation_text.snp5.1:29.4: but do so only in your mind!
translation_text.snp5.1:30.3: he will answer with his voice
translation_text.snp5.1:34.1: Each of them had their own following,
translation_text.snp5.1:34.2: they were renowned the whole world over.
translation_text.snp5.1:34.3: Those attentive ones, <j>meditators who love absorption,
translation_text.snp5.1:34.4: were redolent with the potential <j>of their past deeds.
translation_text.snp5.1:35.2: and circled him to his right,
translation_text.snp5.1:35.3: they set out for the north,
translation_text.snp5.1:36.2: then on to the citadel of Māhissatī;
translation_text.snp5.1:37.2: and the supreme city of Sāvatthī;
translation_text.snp5.1:37.3: on they went to Setavyā and Kapilavatthu,
translation_text.snp5.1:38.1: To Pāvā they went, and Bhoga City,
translation_text.snp5.1:38.2: and on to Vesālī and the Magadhan city.
translation_text.snp5.1:38.3: Finally they reached the Pāsāṇaka Shrine,
translation_text.snp5.1:39.1: Like a thirsty person to cool water,
translation_text.snp5.1:39.2: like a merchant to great profit,
translation_text.snp5.1:39.3: like a heat-struck person to shade,
translation_text.snp5.1:40.2: at the fore of the mendicant Saṅgha,
translation_text.snp5.1:40.3: was teaching the mendicants the Dhamma,
translation_text.snp5.1:40.4: like a lion roaring in the jungle.
translation_text.snp5.1:41.2: like the sun shining with a hundred rays,
translation_text.snp5.1:41.3: like the moon on the fifteenth day
translation_text.snp5.1:41.4: when it has come into its fullness.
translation_text.snp5.1:42.3: Thrilled, he stood to one side
translation_text.snp5.1:42.4: and asked this question in his mind.
translation_text.snp5.1:43.2: of his clan; and his own marks;
translation_text.snp5.1:43.3: what hymns is he proficient in;
translation_text.snp5.1:44.1: “His age is a hundred and twenty.
translation_text.snp5.1:44.2: By clan he is a Bāvari.
translation_text.snp5.1:44.3: There are three marks on his body.
translation_text.snp5.1:44.4: He is a master of the three Vedas,
translation_text.snp5.1:45.1: the teachings on the marks, the testaments,
translation_text.snp5.1:45.3: He teaches recitation to five hundred,
translation_text.snp5.1:45.4: and has reached proficiency in his own teaching.”
translation_text.snp5.1:47.1: “He can cover his face with his tongue;
translation_text.snp5.1:47.2: there is a tuft of hair between his eyebrows;
translation_text.snp5.1:47.3: his private parts are concealed in a foreskin:
translation_text.snp5.1:47.4: know them as this, young man.”
translation_text.snp5.1:49.1: “Who is it that asked a question with their mind?
translation_text.snp5.1:49.2: Was it a god or divinity?
translation_text.snp5.1:49.4: To whom does the Buddha reply?”
translation_text.snp5.1:50.4: and so, O seer, dispel our doubt.”
translation_text.snp5.1:51.3: when joined with faith, mindfulness, and immersion,
translation_text.snp5.1:52.3: arranged his antelope-skin cloak over one shoulder,
translation_text.snp5.1:52.4: and fell with his head to the Buddha’s feet.
translation_text.snp5.1:53.4: bows to your feet, O Clear-eyed One!”
translation_text.snp5.1:54.3: And may you, too, be happy!
translation_text.snp5.1:54.4: May you live long, young man.
translation_text.snp5.1:55.2: I grant the opportunity to clear up all doubt.
translation_text.snp5.1:56.1: Granted the opportunity by the Buddha,
translation_text.snp5.1:56.2: they sat down with joined palms.

variant_text.snp5.1:14.4: Jinānaṁ hettha → jinānaṁ hettha (bj); jinānaṁ heta (si); jinānaṁ heta (sya-all, pts-vp-pli1)
variant_text.snp5.1:15.2: pathavimaṇḍale → puthuvi maṇḍale (bj); puthavimaṇḍale (bj, pts-vp-pli1); paṭhavimaṇḍale (sya-all)
variant_text.snp5.1:20.5: gantvāna passemu → gantvā namassemu (bj, sya-all, pts-vp-pli1)
variant_text.snp5.1:25.3: Dvattiṁsāni ca → dvattiṁsā ca (si, sya-all, pts-vp-pli1); dvattiṁsa tāni (?)
variant_text.snp5.1:36.2: Purimāhissatiṁ → purimaṁ māhissatiṁ (bj, pts-vp-pli1); puraṁ māhissatiṁ (sya-all)
variant_text.snp5.1:41.2: Sataraṁsiṁva → vītaraṁsiṁva (si, sya-all); vītaraṁsiva (pts-vp-pli1); sataraṁsīva (mr); pītaraṁsiṁva (cnd1:42 [Vatthugāthā])
